unit.name: Controlled study: concept bottlenecks and intervention graphs in RL
unit.description: Builds concept-bottleneck multi-agent systems so specific concepts can be intervened on, then uses intervention outcomes to infer coordination patterns and hidden social dynamics.
unit.summary: Kim next switches from pure observation to control by extending concept bottleneck models to reinforcement learning, training agents to solve the task while also predicting explicit concepts so those concepts can be ablated at inference time [ts=2648s] [ts=2701s] [ts=2718s]. In Overcooked, the setup distinguishes coordinated from non-coordinated environments, and intervention analyses reveal that orientation—not position—is especially important because it gives the earliest cue about another agent’s next move [ts=2774s] [ts=2839s] [ts=2923s]. The same framework can also expose lazy agents whose interventions do not affect rewards [ts=2945s] [ts=2963s]. In the more complex Cleanup game, raw intervention statistics suggest one agent mainly influences another, but a graph built from intervention outcomes reveals a subtler relationship structure and shows that apparent dependencies can arise accidentally from the environment rather than genuine coordination [ts=3036s] [ts=3142s] [ts=3211s]. She closes by noting that the controllable model can match baseline performance, but it depends on having or constructing useful human-interpretable concepts such as position and orientation [ts=3270s] [ts=3285s].

key_points.text: A concept bottleneck inserts interpretable variables into each agent so concepts can be manipulated during inference.
Training optimizes task reward together with agreement between predicted concepts and true concepts.
In Overcooked, stronger reward degradation under intervention indicates coordination between agents.
Agent orientation is more informative than position because it signals the other agent’s next move.
Intervention graphs over agent-concept nodes can reveal true inter-agent relationships and expose misleading surface statistics.
The controllable system can perform comparably to the original RL system, but it requires labeled or otherwise available concepts.

In Overcooked, the setup distinguishes coordinated from non-coordinated environments, and intervention analyses reveal that orientation—not position—is especially important because it gives the earliest cue about another agent’s next move [ts=2774s] [ts=2839s] [ts=2923s]. The same framework can also expose lazy agents whose interventions do not affect rewards [ts=2945s] [ts=2963s]. In the more complex Cleanup game, raw intervention statistics suggest one agent mainly influences another, but a graph built from intervention outcomes reveals a subtler relationship structure and shows that apparent dependencies can arise accidentally from the environment rather than genuine coordination [ts=3036s] [ts=3142s] [ts=3211s]. She closes by noting that the controllable model can match baseline performance, but it depends on having or constructing useful human-interpretable concepts such as position and orientation [ts=3270s] [ts=3285s].

kp_catalog.name: Concept bottlenecks for controllable RL interventions
kp_catalog.description: Embedding explicit concepts inside agents enables targeted intervention studies that expose coordination structure and causal dependencies.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because concept bottlenecks for controllable RL interventions clarifies embedding explicit concepts inside agents enables targeted intervention studies that expose coordination structure and causal dependencies, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Inter-agent relationship graphs from interventions
kp_catalog.description: Graphs built from intervention outcomes can reveal hidden social or coordination structure that raw statistics miss.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because inter-agent relationship graphs from interventions clarifies graphs built from intervention outcomes can reveal hidden social or coordination structure that raw statistics miss, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Observational discovery of emergent RL behaviors
kp_catalog.description: Latent-variable modeling of state-action traces can reveal recurring multi-agent behaviors without hand labeling every trajectory.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because observational discovery of emergent RL behaviors clarifies latent-variable modeling of state-action traces can reveal recurring multi-agent behaviors without hand labeling every trajectory, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
